learning_outcomes.description: Understand the complexity of migration experiences, and understand the meaning and significance of key concepts in the field such as ethnicity, migration, nation, nationalism, race and transnationalism;

Apply theoretical and conceptual knowledge acquired in this course in relation to various social and cultural contexts, and develop critical thinking and the ability to analytically conceptualise social problems.
Discuss, write about and apply methodological and theoretical approaches to  migration, culture and identity.

assessments.task_description:
Tutorial Reading Summaries:

Students must submit a short 1 page (200-250 word) piece of written work to demonstrate an engagement with that week's topic to be submitted to the lecturer in the seminar. 

There are eleven weeks of seminars for this task, but students only need to submit eight summaries (the student chooses which eight). The final week can be used as a catch up.

The summaries can take one of two forms:

  • A summary and critical discussion of one of that week's readings (as listed on Blackboard). OR
  • A summary and discussion of a newspaper story from recent months which relates to the week's topic. The discussion will need to draw out ideas and themes from the readings/lecture and be clearly relevant to the topic. A copy of the newspaper article must be attached to the student's summary.

Students can do any mix of reading and newspaper summaries as long as they provide one or the other in each seminar.

Each summary is worth 2.5% of the student's overall grade and is marked as pass/fail.

NB. Formal feedback is not provided on these written summaries.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: To be submitted to the lecturer in class in the Seminar Weeks 2 - 13 inclusive.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
1st presentation 18/08/2026

2nd presentation 24/08/2026 - 26/10/2026

1st presentation will be prepared and delivered in week 4 Seminar, the 2nd presentation in the following weeks.
assessments.task_description:
You'll create two short presentations using the Ignite format to share key insights from migration anthropology research. This assignment develops your ability to communicate complex ideas clearly and concisely, an essential skill for your studies and career.

Ignite is a presentation style used by academics to engage general audiences. Your presentations will use 12 slides that automatically advance every 15 seconds for a total of 3 minutes. There's no stopping or going back once you start. The slides should be image-focused with minimal text, and you'll present live in class whilst being recorded via Echo360. Each presentation will be followed by a Q&A session with the class.

For your research phase, you'll read assigned journal articles and book chapters on migration anthropology, then choose one specific focus to highlight, such as a particular theorist, migration history in one country, or a specific migration practice. We'll discuss topic options in the first seminar. You'll then present during the week that matches your chosen topic, focusing on transmitting crucial information both verbally and visually, and prepare for follow-up questions from the class.

You'll complete two presentations this semester. The first presentation is worth 15% of your final grade and is due in the week 4 seminar, and the second presentation is worth 25%. Due dates for the latter will be negotiated in the first two weeks of semester. This assignment teaches you to synthesise complex academic material, communicate clearly under time constraints, use visual storytelling effectively, and present confidently to an audience.

Format details will be covered in the first seminar.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: To be submitted and delivered in class
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In this assessment you’ll use LEGO Serious Play to explore how borders, nations and belonging are socially constructed, then reflect on that experience anthropologically. The task develops your ability to translate abstract theory into material form and to analyse your own creative practice critically, skills that sit at the heart of anthropological thinking.

LEGO Serious Play is a facilitated, hands-on method in which building with LEGO becomes a way of thinking through complex ideas. Working in small teams, you’ll be assigned a border scenario, for example, a newly independent nation, a contested borderland, a securitised frontier, an economic border zone or a cultural borderland. Together your team will build a diorama that represents how your border is produced, performed and experienced, making deliberate choices about space, symbols, figures and infrastructure to convey anthropological meaning.

The building takes place in class. As you work, document your diorama through photographs and brief notes on the choices your team makes and why. You’ll then write an individual two-page reflection that analyses your team’s border scene using anthropological theories and concepts discussed in class, such as imagined communities, banal nationalism, performative borders, or the social construction of boundaries, and consider what the material method reveals and what it obscures.

This assessment is worth 40% of your final grade. The in-class build and documentation is in the first part of the session, and the reflection written individually in class immediately afterwards.

This assignment teaches you to apply theory to material culture, work collaboratively, analyse spatial and symbolic arrangements ethnographically, and reflect critically on method. Scenario allocation, team formation and format details will be covered in the seminar.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Papers are handed in at the end of the seminar session at 2pm. Students will have ample time to complete the task and students may leave when they have written at least 2 pages for their reflection.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
